quizQuestion: Who directed Citizen Kane in 1941?
explanation: Orson Welles directed, produced, co-wrote, and starred in Citizen Kane at age 25 — an extraordinary debut that introduced groundbreaking cinematography techniques. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Citizen Kane (1941) was directed by Orson Welles, who also starred in and co-wrote the film.
wowFactor: Orson Welles made one of cinema's greatest films as his very first feature, at age 25.

quizQuestion: Which 1941 film opens with the dying word 'Rosebud'?
explanation: 'Rosebud' is the enigmatic last word of Charles Foster Kane in Citizen Kane (1941). The word refers to his childhood sled, symbolizing lost innocence. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Citizen Kane (1941) famously opens with the dying word 'Rosebud', one of cinema's greatest mysteries.
wowFactor: 'Rosebud' — the most famous last word in cinema history — turns out to be the name of a sled.

variants.question: What is the mysterious dying word in Citizen Kane?

quizQuestion: Which 1942 film features the line 'Here's looking at you, kid'?
explanation: 'Here's looking at you, kid' is spoken by Rick (Humphrey Bogart) to Ilsa (Ingrid Bergman) in Casablanca (1942). The American Film Institute ranks it #5 among the 100 greatest film quotes. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Casablanca (1942) gave us 'Here's looking at you, kid' — one of the most quoted lines in film history.
wowFactor: 'Here's looking at you, kid' was reportedly improvised by Bogart during production.

quizQuestion: Who directed the 1942 classic film Casablanca?
explanation: Michael Curtiz, a Hungarian-American director, helmed Casablanca (1942), which won him the Academy Award for Best Director. He is known for his prolific Hollywood career spanning the studio era. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Casablanca (1942) was directed by Michael Curtiz, who won the Academy Award for Best Director.
wowFactor: Michael Curtiz spoke with such a thick Hungarian accent that actors often couldn't understand his direction on set.

variants.question: Michael Curtiz directed which beloved 1942 wartime romance?

quizQuestion: Which 1939 film won a record-setting eight Academy Awards at the time?
explanation: Gone with the Wind (1939) won eight Academy Awards at the 12th Academy Awards ceremony, a record at the time, including Best Picture, Best Director (Victor Fleming), and Best Actress (Vivien Leigh). Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Gone with the Wind (1939) won a then-record eight Academy Awards, including Best Picture.
wowFactor: Gone with the Wind held the record for most Oscar wins for over two decades until Ben-Hur tied it in 1959.

variants.question: Victor Fleming directed which 1939 Civil War epic?

quizQuestion: Which 1939 film famously switches from black-and-white to Technicolor?
explanation: The Wizard of Oz (1939) opens in sepia-toned black-and-white in Kansas, then transitions to vibrant Technicolor when Dorothy arrives in Oz — one of cinema's most celebrated visual storytelling techniques. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: The Wizard of Oz (1939) used a shift from black-and-white to Technicolor to transport Dorothy to a magical world.
wowFactor: The Wizard of Oz's shift from sepia to Technicolor was a deliberate storytelling choice — but it nearly didn't happen due to the budget.

quizQuestion: Who directed It's a Wonderful Life in 1946?
explanation: Frank Capra directed It's a Wonderful Life (1946), starring James Stewart as George Bailey. The film initially underperformed at the box office but became one of the most beloved films in American history through television reruns. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: It's a Wonderful Life (1946) was directed by Frank Capra, starring James Stewart as George Bailey.
wowFactor: It's a Wonderful Life was a box-office flop in 1946 — it only became iconic after its copyright lapsed and TV stations played it constantly.

quizQuestion: Which 1960 Hitchcock film features the famous shower scene?
explanation: The shower scene in Psycho (1960) is one of the most analyzed scenes in cinema history. Janet Leigh's character Marion Crane is killed by Norman Bates in what took 7 days to film with 77 different camera angles. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Psycho (1960) contains the infamous shower scene — 77 camera angles edited together in 3 terrifying minutes.
wowFactor: The shower scene in Psycho took 7 days to film and used 77 different camera angles — yet no nudity is actually shown.

variants.question: Alfred Hitchcock directed which 1960 horror landmark?

quizQuestion: Which actress played Marion Crane in Psycho (1960)?
explanation: Janet Leigh played Marion Crane in Alfred Hitchcock's Psycho (1960). Her performance earned her a Golden Globe Award and an Academy Award nomination for Best Supporting Actress. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Janet Leigh earned an Oscar nomination for playing the doomed Marion Crane in Psycho (1960).
wowFactor: Janet Leigh was so traumatized by Psycho that she refused to take showers for the rest of her life.

quizQuestion: Who co-directed Singin' in the Rain alongside Gene Kelly?
explanation: Singin' in the Rain (1952) was co-directed by Gene Kelly and Stanley Donen. Donen would go on to direct other classics including Funny Face and Charade. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Singin' in the Rain (1952) was co-directed by Gene Kelly and Stanley Donen.
wowFactor: Stanley Donen was only 27 years old when he co-directed Singin' in the Rain, often considered the greatest movie musical ever made.

quizQuestion: Which Billy Wilder film ends with 'Nobody's perfect'?
explanation: Some Like It Hot (1959) ends when Osgood Fielding III (Joe E. Brown) reveals his indifference to Jerry/Daphne being a man with the immortal line 'Nobody's perfect!' — ranked #48 by the AFI among the greatest movie quotes. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Some Like It Hot (1959) ends with the punch line 'Nobody's perfect!' — one of comedy's greatest final lines.
wowFactor: 'Nobody's perfect!' reportedly came from a throwaway improvisation that Billy Wilder kept because he couldn't think of a better ending.

quizQuestion: Which actor played T.E. Lawrence in the 1962 epic film?
explanation: Peter O'Toole starred as T.E. Lawrence in Lawrence of Arabia (1962), directed by David Lean. The performance launched O'Toole to international stardom and earned him the first of eight Academy Award nominations. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Peter O'Toole's portrayal of T.E. Lawrence in Lawrence of Arabia (1962) launched his career and earned him his first Oscar nomination.
wowFactor: Peter O'Toole was nominated for eight Academy Awards without ever winning — a record for nominations without a win.

quizQuestion: Which British director helmed Lawrence of Arabia in 1962?
explanation: David Lean directed Lawrence of Arabia (1962), which won seven Academy Awards including Best Picture and Best Director. Lean also directed The Bridge on the River Kwai (1957) and Doctor Zhivago (1965). Source: Wikipedia.
statement: David Lean directed both Lawrence of Arabia (1962) and The Bridge on the River Kwai (1957), winning Best Director Oscars for both.
wowFactor: David Lean won Best Director twice in five years — for The Bridge on the River Kwai (1957) and Lawrence of Arabia (1962).

quizQuestion: Which actress played Maria von Trapp in The Sound of Music (1965)?
explanation: Julie Andrews starred as Maria von Trapp in The Sound of Music (1965), directed by Robert Wise. The film became the highest-grossing film of 1965 and won five Academy Awards including Best Picture. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Julie Andrews played Maria von Trapp in The Sound of Music (1965), which became the highest-grossing film of that year.
wowFactor: Julie Andrews won back-to-back Best Actress Oscars in 1965 (Mary Poppins) and was nominated again for The Sound of Music the following year.

quizQuestion: West Side Story (1961) was inspired by which Shakespeare play?
explanation: West Side Story (1961), directed by Robert Wise and Jerome Robbins, is a modern retelling of Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet, set among rival gangs in 1950s New York. The music was composed by Leonard Bernstein. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: West Side Story (1961) transposed Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet to rival street gangs in 1950s New York.
wowFactor: West Side Story won 10 Academy Awards in 1962 — a record that stood until Titanic (1997) tied it.

quizQuestion: How many Academy Awards did Ben-Hur (1959) win?
explanation: Ben-Hur (1959), directed by William Wyler, won 11 Academy Awards — a record that stood for 37 years until Titanic (1997) matched it. The film starred Charlton Heston. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Ben-Hur (1959) won 11 Academy Awards, a record that stood for nearly four decades.
wowFactor: Ben-Hur's 11 Oscar wins stood as the all-time record for 37 years until Titanic and The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King matched it.

quizQuestion: Which actor starred in Hitchcock's Vertigo (1958)?
explanation: James Stewart starred in Vertigo (1958), playing retired detective John 'Scottie' Ferguson who develops an obsession with a woman he is hired to follow. Stewart appeared in four Hitchcock films in total. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: James Stewart played acrophobic detective Scottie Ferguson in Hitchcock's Vertigo (1958).
wowFactor: Vertigo was considered a minor Hitchcock film at release — critics now regularly rank it the greatest film ever made.

quizQuestion: Which Hitchcock film is set almost entirely inside one apartment?
explanation: Rear Window (1954) confines its viewpoint almost entirely to photographer L.B. Jefferies' apartment, as he spies on neighbors through his rear window and becomes convinced he witnessed a murder. The entire film reflects his limited perspective. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Rear Window (1954) unfolds almost entirely from one apartment window, as James Stewart watches neighbors and suspects a murder.
wowFactor: Rear Window's entire story unfolds from one room — Hitchcock made voyeurism itself the subject of the film.

quizQuestion: Which 1950 film noir opens with a dead man narrating from a swimming pool?
explanation: Sunset Boulevard (1950), directed by Billy Wilder, opens with its protagonist Joe Gillis (William Holden) narrating while floating dead in a Hollywood swimming pool — a shocking and iconic storytelling device. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Sunset Boulevard (1950) opens with the narrator already dead, floating in a Hollywood swimming pool.
wowFactor: Sunset Boulevard's twist is revealed in the opening shot — the narrator is a corpse — making every line a dead man's confession.

quizQuestion: Which film contains 'I'm ready for my close-up, Mr. DeMille'?
explanation: The line 'All right, Mr. DeMille, I'm ready for my close-up' is spoken by Norma Desmond (Gloria Swanson) in Sunset Boulevard (1950). It is ranked #7 on the AFI's 100 greatest movie quotes list. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: 'I'm ready for my close-up, Mr. DeMille' — Norma Desmond's delusional last words in Sunset Boulevard (1950).
wowFactor: The real Cecil B. DeMille appears in Sunset Boulevard playing himself — making Norma's final delusion even more haunting.

quizQuestion: Which 1954 film features Brando's 'I coulda been a contender' speech?
explanation: 'I could've been a contender' is spoken by Terry Malloy (Marlon Brando) to his brother Charley in a taxi scene in On the Waterfront (1954), directed by Elia Kazan. The AFI ranks it as the #3 greatest movie quote of all time. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: On the Waterfront (1954) contains Brando's legendary 'I coulda been a contender' speech — ranked #3 in movie quote history.
wowFactor: The 'contender' speech was filmed in a fake taxi cab interior on a studio lot — the streets of New York seen through the window were a rear projection.

quizQuestion: Which director made his debut with 12 Angry Men (1957)?
explanation: 12 Angry Men (1957) was Sidney Lumet's feature directorial debut. Adapted from Reginald Rose's 1954 teleplay, it was shot in 19 days entirely in one jury room and earned Academy Award nominations for Best Picture, Best Director, and Best Screenplay. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Sidney Lumet made his feature film directing debut with 12 Angry Men (1957), shot in just 19 days.
wowFactor: 12 Angry Men was shot in only 19 days on a single set — and received Oscar nominations for Best Picture, Director, and Screenplay.

quizQuestion: Which David Lean film features soldiers whistling 'Colonel Bogey March'?
explanation: The Bridge on the River Kwai (1957) famously opens with British POWs whistling 'Colonel Bogey March' as they march into a Japanese prison camp. Alec Guinness won the Best Actor Oscar for his role as Colonel Nicholson. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: The Bridge on the River Kwai (1957) opens with British POWs whistling 'Colonel Bogey March' — now inseparable from the film.
wowFactor: Alec Guinness won the Oscar for Best Actor in Bridge on the River Kwai — and reportedly hated the film until critics praised it.

quizQuestion: Which actress played Holly Golightly in Breakfast at Tiffany's (1961)?
explanation: Audrey Hepburn played Holly Golightly in Breakfast at Tiffany's (1961), directed by Blake Edwards. Her performance, paired with Henry Mancini's Oscar-winning song 'Moon River', became iconic. Several major stars including Marilyn Monroe were considered for the role. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Audrey Hepburn played the enchanting Holly Golightly in Breakfast at Tiffany's (1961), making the little black dress and tiara iconic.
wowFactor: Marilyn Monroe was Truman Capote's first choice for Holly Golightly — but Audrey Hepburn's performance became one of cinema's most iconic.

quizQuestion: What Oscar-winning song was featured in Breakfast at Tiffany's (1961)?
explanation: Henry Mancini composed 'Moon River' for Breakfast at Tiffany's (1961), with lyrics by Johnny Mercer. Audrey Hepburn performed it in the film. The song won the Academy Award for Best Original Song at the 34th Academy Awards. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: 'Moon River' by Henry Mancini won the Oscar for Best Original Song for Breakfast at Tiffany's (1961).
wowFactor: Studio executives wanted to cut 'Moon River' from Breakfast at Tiffany's — Audrey Hepburn reportedly responded with fury and the song stayed.

quizQuestion: Which actor played Atticus Finch in To Kill a Mockingbird (1962)?
explanation: Gregory Peck won the Academy Award for Best Actor for his portrayal of Atticus Finch in To Kill a Mockingbird (1962), directed by Robert Mulligan. Atticus Finch was later named the greatest hero in American film history by the AFI. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Gregory Peck won Best Actor for playing Atticus Finch in To Kill a Mockingbird (1962) — the AFI's greatest film hero.
wowFactor: Gregory Peck said Atticus Finch was his favorite role of his career — and Harper Lee gave him her father's pocket watch as a token of gratitude.

quizQuestion: Who wrote the novel that To Kill a Mockingbird (1962) was adapted from?
explanation: To Kill a Mockingbird (1962) was adapted from Harper Lee's 1960 Pulitzer Prize-winning novel of the same name. The novel was Lee's only published work for 55 years until Go Set a Watchman appeared in 2015. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: To Kill a Mockingbird (1962) was based on Harper Lee's Pulitzer Prize-winning 1960 novel — her only published book for 55 years.
wowFactor: Harper Lee and Truman Capote were childhood friends — and Capote is thought to be the basis for the character Dill in the novel.

quizQuestion: In what year was Gone with the Wind released?
explanation: Gone with the Wind had its world premiere in Atlanta, Georgia on December 15, 1939, and was widely released that year. Directed by Victor Fleming, it is based on Margaret Mitchell's 1936 novel. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Gone with the Wind premiered in Atlanta, Georgia in December 1939.
wowFactor: Gone with the Wind's Atlanta premiere was treated like a state event — the governor declared a three-day holiday.

quizQuestion: How many Academy Awards did West Side Story (1961) win?
explanation: West Side Story (1961) won 10 Academy Awards out of 11 nominations, including Best Picture, Best Director (Robert Wise and Jerome Robbins), and Best Supporting Actress (Rita Moreno). This record stood until Titanic (1997) matched it. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: West Side Story (1961) won 10 Academy Awards, a record matched only decades later.
wowFactor: West Side Story won 10 Oscars out of 11 nominations in 1962 — an extraordinary 91% hit rate.

quizQuestion: In what year was Hitchcock's Vertigo released?
explanation: Vertigo was released in 1958, based on the 1954 French novel D'entre les morts (From Among the Dead). It was reissued in 1983 after years of being suppressed by Hitchcock's estate, and has since risen to the top of many greatest films lists. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Vertigo (1958) was withheld from circulation for decades and only became recognized as a masterpiece after its 1983 re-release.
wowFactor: Hitchcock personally withdrew Vertigo from distribution in 1973 — it wasn't widely seen again until 1983, after he died.
